---
title: "Dublin Data Science Workshop on Survival Analysis"
author: "Mick Cooney <mcooney@describedata.com>"
date: "Last updated: `r format(Sys.time(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
editor: source
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
  error: false
format:
  html:
    light: superhero
    dark: darkly
    anchor-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    number-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    fig-width: 11
    fig-height: 8

---


```{r import_libraries}
#| echo: false

library(conflicted)
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(cowplot)
library(magrittr)
library(rlang)
library(purrr)
library(furrr)
library(glue)
library(fs)
library(survival)
library(survminer)
library(muhaz)
library(broom)
library(arrow)
library(qs)

source("lib_utils.R")

conflict_lst <- resolve_conflicts(
  c("magrittr", "rlang", "dplyr", "readr", "purrr", "ggplot2", "MASS",
    "fitdistrplus")
  )

options(
  width    = 80L,
  warn     = 1,
  mc.cores = parallelly::availableCores()
  )

Sys.setenv(OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS = 8)

set.seed(42)

theme_set(theme_cowplot())

plan(multisession)
```


The notebook begins by loading all required packages, resolving namespace
conflicts for reproducibility, and configuring multi-threaded Arrow I/O. We
also establish global options (line width, cores) and set a consistent theme.
Parallel and timing infrastructure are enabled up-front so later chunks
automatically benefit from these settings without repeated boilerplate.

```{r setup_chunk_timing}
#| echo: false
#| message: false

# Enable chunk timing for all chunks
knitr::opts_chunk$set(time_it = TRUE)

# Create a list to store chunk times
chunk_times <- list()

# Create a hook to capture chunk execution times
knitr::knit_hooks$set(time_it = function(before, options) {
  if (before) {
    # Record start time in global environment
    assign(".chunk_start_time", Sys.time(), envir = .GlobalEnv)
  } else {
    # Calculate elapsed time
    elapsed <- difftime(
        Sys.time(), get(".chunk_start_time", envir = .GlobalEnv),
        units = "secs"
        ) |>
      as.numeric()
    
    # Store in global list
    chunk_times <- get("chunk_times", envir = .GlobalEnv)
    chunk_times[[options$label]] <- elapsed
    assign("chunk_times", chunk_times, envir = .GlobalEnv)
    
    # Print timing message
    message(glue("⏱️  Chunk '{options$label}': {round(elapsed, 2)} seconds"))
  }
})
```




# Basic Concepts

In *survival analysis* we analyse *time-to-event* data and try to estimate
the underlying distribution of times for events to occur. Survival analysis
is a fundamental statistical methodology that deals with the analysis of
duration until an event of interest occurs. While the terminology comes from
medical applications where the event is typically death, the methods are
widely applicable across many domains: customer churn in business, equipment
failure in engineering, time to promotion in organizational studies, and many
others.

The key distinguishing feature of survival analysis is its ability to handle
**censored data** — situations where we know an observation period has ended
but the event of interest has not occurred (or has occurred before observation
began). This is a common and realistic scenario that standard statistical
methods struggle to handle appropriately.

## Introduction

For the purposes of this workshop, we focus on a single event type, but the
topic is wide and broadly applicable. The fundamental question we ask is:
"Given that an individual has survived up to time $t$, what is the
probability they will survive to some future time $t + s$?" Understanding and
estimating these probabilities allows us to make predictions about future
events and to compare survival experiences across different groups or under
different conditions.


### Workshop Materials

All materials for this workshop is available in my standard GitHub repo:

https://github.com/kaybenleroll/dublin_r_workshops


![book cover](img/asaur_cover.jpg)


The content of this workshop is partly based on the book "Applied Survival
Analysis Using R" by Dirk F. Moore. The data from this book is available from
CRAN via the package 'asaur'.


### Example Datasets

This workshop will use a number of different time-to-event datasets, so we
look at those first.

#### Telco Churn Data

The telco churn data contains some customer usage information on mobile phone
customers and whether or not they left their subscription accounts.

```{r show_telco_churn_data}
#| echo: true

telco_cols <- cols(
  areacode = col_character()
  )

telco_churn_tbl <- read_csv(
  "data/telcochurn.csv",
  col_types = telco_cols
  )


telco_churn_tbl |> glimpse()

telco_churn_tbl |> head()
```


#### Prostate Survival

The prostate survival data is generated data from a cancer survival study.

```{r show_prostate_data}
#| echo: true

prostate_surv_tbl <- asaur::prostateSurvival |>
  as_data_frame()

prostate_surv_tbl |> glimpse()

prostate_surv_tbl |> head()
```

#### Smoker Data

The smoker data is a smoking relapse study involving a number of different
treatments and details on when and if the patient resumed smoking.

```{r show_smoker_data}
#| echo: true

pharmaco_smoker_tbl <- asaur::pharmacoSmoking |>
  as_data_frame()

pharmaco_smoker_tbl |> glimpse()

pharmaco_smoker_tbl |> head()
```



## Basic Principles  

### Data Censoring and Truncation

In the problems we work on, our data observation process does not allow us to
fully observe the variable in question. Instead, our observations are often
*right-censored* - that is, we know the value in question is at least the
observed value, but it may also be higher. Right-censoring occurs when the
observation period ends (perhaps because of study termination, loss to
follow-up, or withdrawal) before the event of interest occurs.

For example, in a clinical trial studying time to recovery, if a patient
withdraws from the study after 100 days without having recovered, we record
their survival time as *at least* 100 days. We don't know if they recovered
on day 101, day 150, or never. This is crucial information that ordinary
regression methods cannot properly incorporate.

Expressing this formally, suppose $T^*$ is the true unobserved time to event,
and $U$ is the time to the censoring event (end of study, loss to follow-up,
or withdrawal), then our observed time $T$, and censoring indicator, $\delta$,
are given by

\begin{eqnarray*}
T      &=& \min(T^*, U) \\
\delta &=& I[T^* \leq U]
\end{eqnarray*}

Here $\delta = 1$ indicates an observed event (uncensored) while $\delta = 0$
indicates right-censoring. The pair $(T, \delta)$ is what we actually observe
in our data.

A less common phenomenon is *left-censoring* - where we observe the event to
be at most a given duration. This may happen in medical studies where
continual observation of the patients is not possible or feasible. For
instance, if we're studying time to infection but only conduct examinations
once per month, we know infections occurred before observation but not exactly
when. Survival analysis methods for left-censored data are more specialized
and less commonly applied in practice.

### Hazard and Survival Functions

Our key goal is to find the survival distribution - the distribution of times
from some given start point to the time of the event. Once we understand this
distribution, we can make inferences about how long events typically take,
compare different groups, and model how covariates affect the rate of events.

Two common ways of specifying this distribution are the *survival function*,
$S(t)$ and the *hazard function*, $\lambda(t)$. These two functions contain
equivalent information but provide different perspectives on the same
underlying phenomenon.

#### The Survival Function

$S(t)$ is the probability of surviving to time $t$, or equivalently, the
probability that the time to event exceeds $t$. It is defined as follows:

$$
S(t) = P(T > t), \, 0 \leq t \leq \infty
$$

Thus, $S(0) = 1$ (everyone survives until time 0) and $S(t)$ decreases
monotonically with increasing time $t$. The survival function approaches 0 as
$t \to \infty$ for proper survival distributions. As it is a probability it is
always non-negative and at most 1.

For example, if $S(5) = 0.8$, this means there is an 80% probability that an
individual survives past time 5. Survival curves are often plotted as step
functions, with a drop at each observed event time.

#### The Hazard Function

Alternatively, we can characterize the survival distribution through the
hazard function, which represents the instantaneous rate of event occurrence
given that the individual has survived to time $t$. More formally,

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\delta \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t + \delta \; | \, T > t)}{\delta}
$$

Intuitively, $\lambda(t) dt$ is approximately the probability that an event
occurs in a small interval $[t, t + dt]$ given that the individual survived to
time $t$. The hazard function is always non-negative and can exceed 1 (unlike
probabilities). It is also called the *intensity function*, *instantaneous
failure rate*, or *force of mortality*.

Different shapes of hazard functions tell us about the underlying process:

  - **Increasing hazard**: The risk of event increases over time (aging effect)
  - **Decreasing hazard**: The risk decreases over time (initial high-risk
    period)
  - **Constant hazard**: The risk remains constant (memoryless or exponential
    process)


### Cumulative Functions

Some analyses make it easier to use the *cumulative hazard function*, which
accumulates the hazard over time up to time $t$:

$$
\Lambda(t) = \int^t_0 \lambda(u) \, du
$$

The cumulative hazard has a nice interpretation: $\Lambda(t)$ represents the
total amount of "risk" that an individual has accumulated by time $t$. It is a
non-decreasing function that starts at $\Lambda(0) = 0$ and increases to
infinity as $t \to \infty$.

A fundamental relationship connects the survival function to the cumulative
hazard function:

$$
S(t) = \exp(- \Lambda(t))
$$

This relationship is important because it shows how the two fundamental
functions are mathematically related. Given one, we can always compute the
other. The cumulative hazard form is often useful in statistical modeling and
estimation because the exponential relationship is well-behaved numerically.

### Relationships Between Functions

The survival function $S(t)$, probability density function $f(t)$, hazard
function $\lambda(t)$, and cumulative hazard $\Lambda(t)$ are all
mathematically related. Understanding these relationships helps us move
between different representations of the survival distribution.

The probability density function is related to the survival function by:

$$
f(t) = -\frac{dS(t)}{dt}
$$

This makes intuitive sense: the density is the rate at which the survival
probability is decreasing.

The hazard function can be expressed in terms of both:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)} = -\frac{d \log S(t)}{dt}
$$

This shows that the hazard is the density of events at time $t$ divided by the
probability of surviving to $t$ - a conditional failure rate.

These relationships allow us to derive any of these functions from any other,
which is useful when different parameterizations are more natural for
different distributions or applications.

```{r show_function_relationships}
#| echo: true

# Demonstrate relationships using exponential distribution with rate 0.01
lambda_const <- 0.01
t_vals <- seq(0, 200, length.out = 200)

# Calculate all functions
S_t <- exp(-lambda_const * t_vals)
f_t <- lambda_const * exp(-lambda_const * t_vals)
h_t <- rep(lambda_const, length(t_vals))
H_t <- lambda_const * t_vals

relationships_tbl <- tibble(
  time = t_vals,
  survival = S_t,
  density = f_t,
  hazard = h_t,
  cumulative_hazard = H_t
  )

# Plot all functions
p1 <- ggplot(relationships_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = survival)) +
  labs(title = "Survival S(t)", y = "S(t)")

p2 <- ggplot(relationships_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = density)) +
  labs(title = "Density f(t)", y = "f(t)")

p3 <- ggplot(relationships_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = hazard)) +
  labs(title = "Hazard λ(t)", y = "λ(t)")

p4 <- ggplot(relationships_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = cumulative_hazard)) +
  labs(title = "Cumulative Hazard Λ(t)", y = "Λ(t)")

plot_grid(p1, p2, p3, p4, nrow = 2, ncol = 2)
```


### Mean and Median Survival

While survival functions provide a complete picture of the survival
distribution, it's often useful to summarize them with single statistics. Two
common summary measures are the *mean survival time* and the *median survival
time*.

#### Mean Survival Time

The mean survival time is the expected value of the distribution, computed
using standard probability definitions. It can be expressed in terms of the
survival function as:

$$
\mu = E(T) = \int^\infty_0 t f(t) \, dt = \int^\infty_0 S(t) \, dt
$$

The right-most expression shows that the mean is simply the area under the
survival curve. For heavily censored data, the mean can be difficult to
estimate reliably, since the tail of the distribution (large times) may not be
well-observed.

#### Median Survival Time

The median survival time, $t_{\text{med}}$, is the time at which exactly half
of the population is expected to have experienced the event. It is defined by
the relationship:

$$
S(t_{\text{med}}) = 0.5
$$

The median is often easier to estimate than the mean, especially when data is
heavily censored, since it only requires knowing the survival probability up
to the 50% point rather than requiring the full distribution. In practice, the
median survival time is often reported and compared across groups because it
is more robust to extreme values and censoring patterns.

#### Other Quantiles

Beyond the median, we can define any quantile of the survival distribution.
The $p$-th quantile $t_p$ is defined as:

$$
S(t_p) = 1 - p
$$

For example, the 25th percentile (first quartile) satisfies $S(t_{0.25}) =
0.75$, meaning 75% of individuals survive past this time. Reporting multiple
quantiles (e.g., quartiles) provides a more complete summary than the median
alone.

```{r demonstrate_quantiles}
#| echo: true

# Create a simple KM estimator for demonstration
tt_demo   <- c(7, 6, 6, 5, 2, 4)
cens_demo <- c(0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1)

demo_tbl <- tibble(tt = tt_demo, cens = cens_demo)

demo_km <- survfit(
  Surv(tt, cens) ~ 1,
  data = demo_tbl,
  conf.type = "log-log"
  )

# Extract quantiles
quantile_times <- quantile(demo_km, probs = c(0.25, 0.50, 0.75))

quantile_times |> print()

# We can also visualize these on the survival curve
quantile_df <- data.frame(
  quantile = c("25th", "50th", "75th"),
  prob = c(0.75, 0.50, 0.25),
  time = as.numeric(quantile_times$quantile)
  )

ggsurvplot(demo_km)$plot +
  geom_hline(
    data = quantile_df,
    aes(yintercept = prob, color = quantile),
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  labs(title = "Survival Curve with Quantiles")
```


## Example Distributions

Let's look at some different survival distributions to build intuition about
how different hazard patterns translate into survival curves. By examining
several stylized examples, we can understand how the shape of the hazard
function affects the survival experience. These examples represent common
patterns seen in real-world applications and help us recognize and interpret
the underlying processes generating time-to-event data.

### Constant Hazard

A constant hazard function represents a situation where the risk of an event
occurring is the same at all times, regardless of how long an individual has
already survived. This is characteristic of the **exponential distribution**
and represents a "memoryless" process - the probability of an event in the
next time interval doesn't depend on how much time has already passed.

In real-world scenarios, constant hazard might approximate:

  - Component failures due to random external shocks (not wear-and-tear)
  - Customer churn in highly competitive markets with stable conditions
  - Certain types of equipment failure where aging effects are minimal

The survival curve for a constant hazard declines exponentially, creating a
smooth curve that never quite reaches zero (though it approaches it
asymptotically).

```{r constant_hazard_plots}
#| echo: true

t_seq <- 1:100

h_const_seq <- rep_along(t_seq, 0.01)
S_const_seq <- cumprod(c(1, (1 - h_const_seq)))


hazard_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = t_seq, y = h_const_seq)) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Instantaneous Hazard")

cuml_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = c(0, t_seq), y = S_const_seq)) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Cumulative Survival")

plot_grid(hazard_plot, cuml_plot, nrow = 2)
```

If we increase the constant hazard, we get a similar shape, but the curve
declines faster. A higher hazard rate means events occur more frequently,
so the survival probability drops more rapidly. Notice that the fundamental
shape remains the same (exponential decay) - only the rate of decline changes.
This demonstrates an important principle: the hazard rate controls the speed
of the survival process, not its qualitative behavior.

```{r constant_hazard_higher_plots}
#| echo: true

h_const_high_seq <- rep_along(t_seq, 0.03)
S_const_high_seq <- cumprod(c(1, (1 - h_const_high_seq)))


hazard_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = t_seq, y = h_const_high_seq)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = t_seq, y = h_const_seq),
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Instantaneous Hazard")

cuml_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = c(0, t_seq), y = S_const_high_seq)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = c(0, t_seq), y = S_const_seq),
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Cumulative Survival")

plot_grid(hazard_plot, cuml_plot, nrow = 2)
```


### Early Hazard

An early hazard pattern, also known as a **decreasing hazard**, occurs when
the risk of an event is highest at the beginning and decreases over time.
This pattern is common in scenarios involving:

  - Post-surgical mortality (highest risk immediately after surgery)
  - Infant mortality patterns in certain diseases
  - "Burn-in" failures in manufacturing (defective items fail quickly)
  - Early customer churn (dissatisfied customers leave quickly, satisfied ones
    stay longer)
  - Equipment with "break-in" periods where initial defects are exposed

The decreasing hazard implies that if an individual survives the early
high-risk period, their subsequent risk of experiencing the event becomes
lower and more stable. This is sometimes called "selection effect" - the
frailer individuals experience events early, leaving a more robust population
as time progresses.

In the survival curve, this manifests as a steep initial drop followed by a
more gradual, flatter decline. The slope of the survival curve decreases over
time, reflecting the reduced hazard.

```{r early_hazard_plots}
#| echo: true

early_seq <- seq(0.10, 0.01, by = -0.01)
late_seq  <- rep(0.01, 100 - length(early_seq))

h_early_seq <- c(early_seq, late_seq)
S_early_seq <- cumprod(c(1, (1 - h_early_seq)))


hazard_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = t_seq, y = h_early_seq)) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Instantaneous Hazard")

cuml_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = c(0, t_seq), y = S_early_seq)) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Cumulative Survival")

plot_grid(hazard_plot, cuml_plot, nrow = 2)
```



### Late Hazard

A late hazard pattern, or **increasing hazard**, represents situations where
the risk of an event grows over time. This is the classic "aging" or
"wear-out" pattern commonly seen in:
  - Age-related mortality in biological systems
  - Mechanical wear and tear (parts degrade over time)
  - Chronic disease progression
  - Long-term customer relationships (satisfaction may erode over time)
  - Material fatigue failures

With an increasing hazard, the longer an individual survives, the higher
their instantaneous risk becomes. This reflects cumulative damage,
deterioration, or depletion of protective factors over time.

The survival curve shows a gentle initial decline that becomes progressively
steeper. Unlike the decreasing hazard case, here the slope increases over
time, meaning the survival probability drops more rapidly as time progresses.
This accelerating decline is characteristic of aging and degradation
processes.

```{r late_hazard_plots}
#| echo: true

late_seq  <- seq(0.01, 0.10, by = 0.0015)
early_seq <- rep(0.01, 100 - length(late_seq))

h_late_seq <- c(early_seq, late_seq)
S_late_seq <- cumprod(c(1, (1 - h_late_seq)))


hazard_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = t_seq, y = h_late_seq)) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Instantaneous Hazard")

cuml_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(aes(x = c(0, t_seq), y = S_late_seq)) +
  expand_limits(y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Cumulative Survival")

plot_grid(hazard_plot, cuml_plot, nrow = 2)
```


# Estimations of the Survival Functions

Having established the theoretical foundations of survival analysis, we now
turn to the practical question: how do we estimate survival and hazard
functions from observed data? This is challenging because of censoring - we
don't observe complete event times for all individuals. Standard statistical
methods like computing empirical cumulative distribution functions don't work
because they would treat censored observations incorrectly.

We'll explore two main approaches: the **Kaplan-Meier estimator** for
survival functions (a non-parametric method that makes minimal assumptions)
and **smoothed hazard estimation** using kernel methods. Understanding both
approaches and how they relate to each other is essential for practical
survival analysis.

## The Kaplan-Meier Estimator

The Kaplan-Meier (KM) estimator, also called the product-limit estimator, is
the gold standard non-parametric method for estimating survival functions
from censored data. Developed independently by Kaplan & Meier (1958), it
handles right-censoring elegantly by updating the survival probability only
at observed event times, properly accounting for the number of individuals
still at risk.

Kaplan-Meier is the standard method for estimating the survival function of a
given dataset. Formally, it is defined as follows

$$
\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \leq t} (1 - \hat{q}_i) = \prod_{t_i \leq t} \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)
$$

where $n_i$ is the number of subjects at risk at time $t_i$ (those who have
neither experienced the event nor been censored before $t_i$), and $d_i$ is
the number of individuals who fail at that time.

The intuition is straightforward: at each event time, we calculate the
conditional probability of surviving past that time given survival up to that
point. The overall survival probability is the product of all these
conditional probabilities. Censored observations contribute to the risk set
until their censoring time, then are removed without affecting the event
count.

### Using Kaplan-Meier

In R, we construct KM estimators using the `survfit()` function from the
`survival` package. The syntax follows R's standard formula interface, making
it easy to estimate survival curves for entire datasets or by groups.

The key function for specifying survival data is `Surv()`, which creates a
survival object from time and event indicator variables. This special object
type ensures that survival analysis functions correctly distinguish between
observed events and censored observations.

Before we move on to our datasets, we start with a small set of data to
illustrate the basic workflow.

```{r km_estimator_intro}
#| echo: true

tt   <- c(7, 6, 6, 5, 2, 4)
cens <- c(0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1)
grp  <- c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1)

Surv(tt, cens)

sample_tbl <- tibble(tt = tt, cens = cens, grp = grp)

example_km <- survfit(
  Surv(tt, cens) ~ 1,
  data = sample_tbl,
  conf.type = "log-log"
  )

plot(example_km)
```

Basic plotting routines are worth trying, but the `survminer` package has
specialised plots that use `ggplot2` to create them. The `ggsurvplot()`
function provides much richer visualizations with confidence intervals, risk
tables, and customization options that follow ggplot2 conventions.

```{r km_estimator_survminer}
#| echo: true

ggsurvplot(example_km)
```

Printing out the 'fitted' object gives us some basic statistics including
the number of observations, number of events (failures), median survival
time, and confidence intervals for the median.

```{r km_estimator_print}
#| echo: true

example_km |> print()
```

We get more details from the `summary()` function, which provides estimates
at each event time including: the number at risk ($n_i$), number of events
($d_i$), survival probability ($\hat{S}(t)$), and confidence bounds. This
table is the core output of the Kaplan-Meier method and shows exactly how the
survival probability is updated at each observed failure time.

```{r km_estimator_summary}
#| echo: true

example_km |> summary()
```

#### Exercises

  1. Construct the KM estimator for the telco churn data
  1. What is the median survival time for this data?
  1. What is the mean survival time?
  1. Repeat the above for the other two datasets.


### Confidence Intervals for Kaplan-Meier

The Kaplan-Meier estimator is a statistic, and like all statistics, it has
uncertainty. Confidence intervals quantify this uncertainty and help us
understand the precision of our survival estimates.

There are several methods for constructing confidence intervals for $S(t)$:

1. **Plain (linear)**: Uses normal approximation to $\hat{S}(t)$ directly
2. **Log transformation**: Uses normal approximation to $\log(\hat{S}(t))$
3. **Log-log transformation**: Uses normal approximation to
   $\log(-\log(\hat{S}(t)))$

The log-log method is generally preferred because:

  - It respects the constraint that $0 \leq S(t) \leq 1$
  - It performs better for small sample sizes
  - It has better coverage properties in simulation studies

In R, we specify the confidence interval type using the `conf.type` argument:

```{r km_confidence_intervals}
#| echo: true

# Compare different confidence interval methods
km_plain <- survfit(
  Surv(tt, cens) ~ 1,
  data = sample_tbl,
  conf.type = "plain"
  )

km_log <- survfit(
  Surv(tt, cens) ~ 1,
  data = sample_tbl,
  conf.type = "log"
  )

km_loglog <- survfit(
  Surv(tt, cens) ~ 1,
  data = sample_tbl,
  conf.type = "log-log"
  )

# Extract and compare
plain_tbl <- km_plain |>
  tidy() |>
  mutate(method = "plain")

log_tbl <- km_log |>
  tidy() |>
  mutate(method = "log")

loglog_tbl <- km_loglog |>
  tidy() |>
  mutate(method = "log-log")

ci_comparison_tbl <- bind_rows(plain_tbl, log_tbl, loglog_tbl)

# Plot comparison
ggplot(ci_comparison_tbl) +
  geom_step(aes(x = time, y = estimate)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(x = time, ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high, fill = method),
    alpha = 0.3
    ) +
  labs(
    title = "Comparison of CI Methods",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Survival Probability"
    )
```

Notice how the different methods can produce slightly different confidence
bands, especially at the extremes of the survival curve. The log-log method
(default in `survfit()`) tends to give more reasonable bounds that stay within
[0, 1].


#### Exercises

  1. Construct the KM estimator for the telco churn data
  1. What is the median survival time for this data?
  1. What is the mean survival time?
  1. Repeat the above for the other two datasets.


### Follow-up Time

A useful measure may be how long the observation period lasts, something that
can be subtly difficult to measure. Understanding follow-up time is important
because it tells us about the quality and completeness of our data - longer
follow-up generally provides more reliable estimates, especially in the tail
of the distribution.

One clever method to estimate follow-up time is to "reverse" the event and
censoring indicators - that is, we consider the original event as a censoring
of the observation in the study, and censored observations as "events"
(study completion). This reverse Kaplan-Meier approach gives us the
distribution of observation times accounting for the fact that some
observations end because of events (which we now treat as censored).

```{r measure_followup_time}
#| echo: true

sample_tbl <- sample_tbl |>
  mutate(follow = 1 - cens)

follow_km <- survfit(
  Surv(tt, follow) ~ 1,
  data = sample_tbl
  )

ggsurvplot(follow_km)

follow_km |> summary()
```


## Smoothed Hazard Functions

While Kaplan-Meier gives us a non-parametric estimate of the survival
function, we often want to estimate the hazard function directly. The hazard
function can reveal important patterns about the instantaneous risk over time
that may not be immediately obvious from the survival curve.

An empirical estimate of the hazard function at each event time is given by
the proportion of at-risk individuals who experience the event:

$$
\mu(i) = \frac{d_i}{n_i}
$$

This is a very noisy estimate as it is sensitive to sample noise, especially
when the number of events at any given time is small. The resulting hazard
estimate jumps around erratically and makes it difficult to discern underlying
patterns or trends in the instantaneous risk.

### The Hazard Estimator

To obtain more smooth functions we use kernel density estimator techniques.
Kernel smoothing applies a weighted moving average across time, producing a
continuous, smooth estimate of the hazard function. The `muhaz` package
implements these methods, with options for different kernels and bandwidth
selection strategies.

The smoothing helps us see trends and patterns in the hazard function that
would be obscured by the noise in the raw empirical estimates. However, this
comes at the cost of introducing bias - we're trading variance for bias to
achieve interpretable estimates.

```{r show_muhaz_plots}
#| echo: true

sample_muhaz <- muhaz(sample_tbl$tt, sample_tbl$cens, max.time = 7)

plot(sample_muhaz)
```

`broom` has tidying methods for `muhaz()` and this allows us to create plots
with `ggplot2`. The `tidy()` function converts the muhaz object into a tidy
tibble with time and hazard estimate columns, making it easy to work with
using tidyverse tools.

```{r show_muhaz_tidy}
#| echo: true

muhaz_tidy_tbl <- sample_muhaz |> tidy()

muhaz_tidy_tbl |> glimpse()
```

We have estimates of the hazard function now and so can plot it.

```{r show_muhaz_tidy_plot}
#| echo: true

ggplot(muhaz_tidy_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = estimate)) +
  expand_limits(x = 0, y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Estimated Hazard")
```


#### Exercises

  1. Construct the smoothed estimator for the telco churn data
  1. What time has the highest hazard rate?
  1. What time has the lowest hazard rate?
  1. Repeat the above for the other two datasets.


### Boundary Corrections

By default, `muhaz()` corrects for the boundary on both sides, but we may not
wish this. Boundary corrections adjust the kernel estimator near the edges of
the observed time range (at time 0 and at the maximum observed time) where
the standard kernel approach can produce biased estimates.

Near boundaries, the kernel "window" extends beyond the data range, causing
downward bias. Boundary correction methods attempt to compensate for this.
However, these corrections can sometimes introduce artifacts or instability,
so it's worth comparing corrected and uncorrected estimates.

To get estimates without this correction, we add it as an argument to the
function call using `b.cor = "none"`.


```{r show_muhaz_nocorr}
#| echo: true

sample_nocorr_muhaz <- muhaz(
  sample_tbl$tt,
  sample_tbl$cens,
  max.time = 7,
  b.cor = "none"
  )

plot(sample_nocorr_muhaz)


sample_nocorr_muhaz_tidy_tbl <- sample_nocorr_muhaz |> tidy()

ggplot(sample_nocorr_muhaz_tidy_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = estimate)) +
  expand_limits(x = 0, y = 0) +
  xlab("Time") +
  ylab("Estimated Hazard")
```

To help ensure these smoothed estimates are capturing the correct aspects of
the data, we also have the equivalent `pehaz()` functions - giving histogram
estimates of the hazards. These provide discrete, binned estimates much like
histograms do for density estimation, while `muhaz()` provides continuous
estimates analogous to kernel density estimation. Comparing both can help
validate that the smoothing isn't obscuring important features or introducing
spurious patterns.

#### Exercises

  1. Construct the non-corrected smoothed estimator for the telco churn data
  1. Repeat the above for the other two datasets.


## Comparing KM and Smoothed Estimates

Now that we have both methods of checking the empirical estimates, it is good
to compare them. This comparison serves as a valuable validation check - if
the two methods give substantially different results, it may indicate issues
with the smoothing bandwidth, boundary effects, or data quality.

Before we do this, we note that Kaplan-Meier estimates the survival function
$S(t)$ but `muhaz()` gives us estimates of the hazard function $\lambda(t)$.
To compare them directly, we need to convert one to match the other.

Thus we need to do some conversions and the easiest way to do this is to
numerically integrate the hazard functions to calculate the survival function
using the relationship $S(t) = \exp(-\Lambda(t))$ where $\Lambda(t) =
\int_0^t \lambda(u) du$. We can then compare this derived survival curve to
the KM estimate. Close agreement suggests both methods are capturing the same
underlying survival process.

```{r integrate_muhaz}
#| echo: true

muhaz_surv_tbl <- muhaz_tidy_tbl |>
  mutate(
    dt  = c(0, diff(time)),
    S_t = exp(-cumsum(estimate * dt))
    )

ggsurvplot(example_km)$plot +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = S_t), data = muhaz_surv_tbl)
```


### No Boundary Correction

We also want to compare the results without the boundary correction.

```{r compare_nocorr_estimates}
#| echo: true

muhaz_nocorr_surv_tbl <- sample_nocorr_muhaz_tidy_tbl |>
  mutate(
    dt  = c(0, diff(time)),
    S_t = exp(-cumsum(estimate * dt))
    )

ggsurvplot(example_km)$plot +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = S_t),
    colour = "blue",
    data = muhaz_surv_tbl
    ) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = S_t), data = muhaz_nocorr_surv_tbl)
```

We see that removing the boundary corrections can cause big discrepancies
between the smoothed and discrete estimates (at least in this case). The
uncorrected estimate shows more divergence from the Kaplan-Meier curve,
particularly at the extremes of the time range. This suggests that boundary
corrections are helpful for this dataset, though the optimal choice can vary
depending on data characteristics and analysis goals.


## Estimating Survival Curves for the Telco Churn Data

We now are going to build various Kaplan-Meier estimates for all the data,
as well as splits on the data along `vmailplan` - whether or not the account
had a voice mail, and `intlplan` - whether or not the account had an
international calls plan.

This is a common pattern in survival analysis: we estimate an overall survival
curve for the entire population, then stratify by categorical variables to see
if different groups have different survival experiences. These comparisons can
reveal important risk factors and help us understand which customer
characteristics are associated with longer or shorter account lifetimes.

```{r create_km_fits}
#| echo: true

telco_all_km   <- survfit(Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ 1, data = telco_churn_tbl)
telco_vmail_km <- survfit(Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ vmailplan, data = telco_churn_tbl)
telco_intl_km  <- survfit(Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ intlplan, data = telco_churn_tbl)
```


### Plotting the Curves

Having created the KM estimates, we first plot the curves to check for
differences. Visual inspection of survival curves is often the first step in
identifying potential differences between groups. Look for:

  - Separation between curves (suggesting different survival experiences)
  - Crossing curves (indicating non-proportional hazards)
  - Confidence interval overlap (suggesting statistical uncertainty about
    differences)

```{r plot_km_curves}
#| echo: true

telco_all_km   |> ggsurvplot(censor = FALSE)
telco_vmail_km |> ggsurvplot(censor = FALSE)
telco_intl_km  |> ggsurvplot(censor = FALSE)
```

The estimators comes with upper and lower confidence bounds, and it may be
useful to include these estimates when determining the differences. These
confidence intervals quantify our uncertainty about the true survival
probability at each time point. When confidence intervals for different groups
overlap substantially, it suggests we cannot be confident that the groups
truly differ. We will look at a more formal way of discriminating between
curves (log-rank tests) in a while.

```{r plot_km_curves_bounds}
#| echo: true

telco_all_km   |> ggsurvplot(censor = FALSE, conf.int = TRUE)
telco_vmail_km |> ggsurvplot(censor = FALSE, conf.int = TRUE)
telco_intl_km  |> ggsurvplot(censor = FALSE, conf.int = TRUE)
```


### Combining KM Estimators

This is useful, but it would be better to combine all these together in a
single plot for easier comparison. To do this, we need the tables of the
estimator data, and stack them together.

Fortunately, the *broom* package is compatible with basic survival analysis
routines, so this provides us with utilities to help with this. The `tidy()`
function extracts the survival curve data into a standard tibble format that
we can manipulate with dplyr and plot with ggplot2.

If we need to access the KM data directly, we can use `tidy()` to get a look:

```{r introspect_km_fits}
#| echo: true

telco_all_km_data   <- telco_all_km |> tidy()
telco_all_km_data   |> glimpse()

telco_vmail_km_data <- telco_vmail_km |> tidy()
telco_vmail_km_data |> glimpse()

telco_intl_km_data  <- telco_intl_km |> tidy()
telco_intl_km_data  |> glimpse()
```

We stack the data and create a line plot of each of the survival curves.

```{r plot_stacked_km_plots}
#| echo: true

stacked_km_tbl <- list(
    telco_all_km_data |> mutate(strata = "ALL"),
    telco_vmail_km_data,
    telco_intl_km_data
    ) |>
  bind_rows()


ggplot(stacked_km_tbl) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = estimate, colour = strata)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = conf.high, colour = strata),
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = conf.low, colour = strata),
    linetype = "dashed"
    )
```

This chart is not as useful as it could be - it is too 'busy'. We need to
simplify.

Instead of including everything, we will look at the international plan curves
along with the estimator calculated from the full dataset.

```{r plot_stacked_km_all_intl_plots}
#| echo: true

ggplot(stacked_km_tbl |> filter(grepl("ALL|intl", strata))) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = estimate, colour = strata)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = conf.high, colour = strata),
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = conf.low, colour = strata),
    linetype = "dashed"
    )
```

From this, it looks like people without international plans are similar to
the population as a whole, but those with plans have a much worse churn
effect. This type of information will be useful later.

We can do a similar thing for the voice-mail plans.

```{r plot_stacked_km_all_vmail_plots}
#| echo: true

ggplot(stacked_km_tbl |> filter(grepl("ALL|vmail", strata))) +
  geom_line(aes(x = time, y = estimate, colour = strata)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = conf.high, colour = strata),
    linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(x = time, y = conf.low, colour = strata),
    linetype = "dashed"
    )
```

This split is more mixed, but it appears that those with a voicemail plan have
a better churn experience than the population, but those without are similar
to the population as a whole.


### Testing for Differences: The Log-Rank Test

While visual inspection of survival curves is informative, we need formal
statistical tests to determine whether observed differences between groups are
statistically significant or could be due to random chance. The **log-rank
test** is the standard non-parametric test for comparing survival curves
between two or more groups.

We conduct this test using `survdiff()` from the survival package, with the
null hypothesis that there is no difference in survival between groups. The
next section explores the theoretical foundation and practical applications in
detail.


# Comparing Curves: The Log-Rank Test

Now that we have ways of constructing different survival curves from our
data, we can compare the patterns visually and numerically across groups.
Visualizing survival curves provides the primary insight: do the curves
differ substantially, and if so, where? The log-rank test offers a
computational aid for summarizing observed differences between curves,
though we emphasize visual interpretation and effect sizes over p-values.

## Comparing Curves: Observed vs. Expected

When comparing survival curves across groups, we can ask: do the observed
event counts in each group differ from what we'd see if survival were
identical across groups?

At each time point where an event occurs, we can compute expected event
counts given the population at risk and total events observed:

|            |Control          |    Treatment      |   Total     |
|----------- |:---------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------:|
|Failure     |$d_{0i}$         | $d_{1i}$          | $d_i$       |
|Non-failure |$n_{0i} - d_{0i}$| $n_{1i} - d_{1i}$ | $n_i - d_i$ |
|Total       |$n_{0i}$         | $n_{1i}$          | $n_i$       |

where:

  - $d_{0i}$ and $d_{1i}$ are the number of observed failures in each group at
    time $t_i$
  - $n_{0i}$ and $n_{1i}$ are the number at risk in each group at time $t_i$
  - $d_i$ is the total number of events at time $t_i$

The expected count and variance for the control group are:

\begin{eqnarray*}
e_{0i} &=& E(d_{0i}) = \frac{n_{0i} d_i}{n_i}                                        \\
v_{0i} &=& \text{var}(d_{0i}) = \frac{n_{0i} n_{1i} d_i (n_i - d_i)}{n_i^2(n_i - 1)} 
\end{eqnarray*}

Summing across all time points:

\begin{eqnarray*}
U_0    &=& \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_{0i} - e_{0i})   \\
V_0    &=& \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_{0i}
\end{eqnarray*}

The log-rank test statistic is:

$$
\frac{U_0^2}{V_0} \sim \chi_1^2 \text{ (for two groups)}
$$

This provides a summary measure of the aggregate difference between observed
and expected, but the practical value lies in examining the "Observed" versus
"Expected" columns in the output: which group has more or fewer events than
expected? By how much? That difference is what matters.

### A Note on P-Values

P-values quantify evidence against the null hypothesis of equal survival, but
they are brittle measures that conflate effect size with sample size. With
large samples, trivial differences yield small p-values; with small samples,
large differences may yield large p-values. **The magnitude of the difference
and its practical importance are what matter for decision-making.** Always
examine the curves themselves and the observed vs. expected counts rather than
relying on a p-value threshold (e.g., 0.05) to guide interpretation.

## Comparing Binary Categories

The simplest application is comparing survival across two groups: treatment
versus control, presence or absence of a characteristic, or any other binary
grouping. We examine visual differences in curves and quantify them using
observed versus expected event counts.

### Voicemail Plan Comparison

Let's examine whether a voicemail plan affects churn:

```{r compare_vmail_curves}
#| echo: true

telco_vmail_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ vmailplan,
  data = telco_churn_tbl
  )

telco_vmail_survdiff |> print()
telco_vmail_survdiff |> glance()
```

The "Observed" and "Expected" columns tell the story: customers with a
voicemail plan have substantially fewer observed events (146 observed vs. 185
expected), indicating better retention than would occur under equal survival.
Conversely, customers without a plan have more events than expected (274
observed vs. 235 expected), indicating worse retention.

This aligns visually with the Kaplan-Meier curves: the voicemail plan group
has a noticeably higher survival probability at all time points. The
difference is substantial and practically meaningful for business
decision-making.

### International Plan Comparison

Now let's examine an international calling plan:

```{r compare_intl_curves}
#| echo: true

telco_intl_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ intlplan,
  data = telco_churn_tbl
  )

telco_intl_survdiff |> print()
telco_intl_survdiff |> glance()
```

Here we see customers with international plans have more observed events than
expected (194 vs. 177), indicating worse retention. The gap is smaller than
the voicemail comparison, suggesting a weaker relationship. This could reflect
that international plan adoption is associated with other risk factors (travel
patterns, churn propensity) or simply a smaller underlying effect.

## Comparing Multiple Categories

When comparing more than two groups, we ask the same question: do the
observed event counts differ meaningfully from what we'd expect under equal
survival? Visual inspection becomes even more important with multiple curves,
as differences can be complex and context-dependent.

### Area Code Comparison

Let's examine whether telephone area codes affect churn. First, we check how
many categories we have:

```{r check_area_code_levels}
#| echo: true

telco_churn_tbl |> count(areacode)
```

Three area codes are represented. Let's visualize the Kaplan-Meier curves:

```{r create_areacode_km_estimates}
#| echo: true

telco_areacode_km <- survfit(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ areacode,
  data = telco_churn_tbl
  )

telco_areacode_km |> ggsurvplot(censor = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)
```

The curves track closely together. The observed/expected comparison:

```{r compare_areacode_curves}
#| echo: true

telco_areacode_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ areacode,
  data = telco_churn_tbl
  )

telco_areacode_survdiff |> print()
telco_areacode_survdiff |> glance()
```

All three area codes show observed and expected counts that are quite similar.
The observed/expected difference is negligible across all groups, consistent
with what we see visually. This makes business sense: area codes are broad
geographic regions, unlikely to capture meaningful local effects on churn.


## Discretizing Continuous Variables for Comparison

Many variables affecting survival are continuous (age, income, usage metrics),
yet group comparisons require categorical groupings. We discretize continuous
variables into meaningful bins for exploratory visualization and comparison.
This approach works well for understanding non-linear relationships and
validating patterns, though regression models (covered later) handle continuous
variables more elegantly.

### Rationale for Discretization

Discretization serves several purposes:

  1. **Visualization**: Group comparisons are easier to interpret visually
  1. **Interpretability**: Binned results are more explainable to stakeholders
  1. **Exploration**: Simple binning reveals non-linear or threshold effects
  1. **Robustness**: Test patterns across different discretization schemes to
     ensure findings are stable

The key is choosing sensible cutpoints that reflect domain knowledge and data
distribution.

### Example: Customer Service Calls

We suspect customer satisfaction (measured by calls to service) affects churn.
Let's examine the distribution first:

```{r explore_custservicecalls_distribution}
#| echo: true

telco_churn_tbl |>
  ggplot(aes(x = custservicecalls)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Customer Service Calls",
    x = "Number of Calls",
    y = "Frequency"
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 10, by = 1))
```

The distribution is heavily skewed: most customers make 0-2 calls, with a long
right tail. This skewness means quantile-based binning (equal-sized groups) is
suboptimal. Domain knowledge suggests better cutpoints.

### Domain-Driven Binning

Rather than splitting at quantiles, we use business reasoning:

  - **0-1 calls**: Normal, satisfied customers
  - **2 calls**: Slightly elevated engagement
  - **3-5 calls**: Noticeably concerned customers
  - **6+ calls**: Very dissatisfied customers

This creates natural categories that reflect customer satisfaction levels:

```{r discretize_custservicecalls}
#| echo: true

telco_churn_cat_tbl <- telco_churn_tbl |>
  mutate(
    calls_cat = cut(
      custservicecalls,
      breaks = c(0, 1, 2, 5, Inf),
      right = FALSE,
      labels = c("0-1", "2", "3-5", "6+")
      )
    )

# Verify the discretization
telco_churn_cat_tbl |>
  count(calls_cat) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
```

### Visual Comparison Across Bins

Kaplan-Meier curves for each bin:

```{r create_callscat_km_estimates}
#| echo: true

telco_callscat_km <- survfit(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_cat,
  data = telco_churn_cat_tbl
  )

telco_callscat_km |>
  ggsurvplot(
    censor   = FALSE,
    conf.int = TRUE
    )
```

A clear pattern emerges: customers with fewer calls have much better survival.
The gradient is strong—each increase in call category associates with visibly
worse retention. The 0-1 and 2 categories track similarly (good retention),
while 3-5 and especially 6+ show substantially higher churn.

### Quantifying Group Differences

The observed/expected comparison across bins:

```{r compare_callscat_curves}
#| echo: true

telco_callscat_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_cat,
  data = telco_churn_cat_tbl
  )

telco_callscat_survdiff |> print()
telco_callscat_survdiff |> glance()
```

The observed/expected discrepancies are large and consistent: low-call groups
have substantially fewer events than expected (better retention), while
high-call groups have many more events than expected (worse retention). This
is one of the strongest patterns in the data and is critically important for
the business—customer service call volume is a strong indicator of churn risk.

### Sensitivity to Discretization Choices

An important caveat: results can be sensitive to where you place cutpoints.
The domain-driven binning we used (0-1, 2, 3-5, 6+) reflects business
intuition, but we should test whether the pattern holds under different
discretization schemes. If the finding is robust, it will persist across
multiple approaches; if it's fragile, different binning will yield
contradictory results.

#### Alternative 1: Quantile-Based Binning

The simplest alternative is equal-sized groups via quantiles. This ignores
the distribution but ensures each bin has similar sample size:

```{r discretization_quantile}
#| echo: true

# Create 4 equal-sized groups
telco_churn_quantile_tbl <- telco_churn_tbl |>
  mutate(
    calls_quantile = ntile(custservicecalls, 4) |>
      factor(labels = c("Q1 (lowest)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (highest)"))
    )

# Check group sizes
telco_churn_quantile_tbl |>
  count(calls_quantile) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
```

Visualize the survival curves:

```{r plot_quantile_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callsquantile_km <- survfit(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_quantile,
  data = telco_churn_quantile_tbl
  )

telco_callsquantile_km |>
  ggsurvplot(
    censor   = FALSE,
    conf.int = TRUE
    )
```

Compare observed vs. expected:

```{r compare_quantile_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callsquantile_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_quantile,
  data = telco_churn_quantile_tbl
  )

telco_callsquantile_survdiff |> print()
```

The gradient persists: higher quantiles show progressively worse survival.
However, quantile binning obscures the true cutpoints—Q1 might range from 0-1
calls while Q4 might be 4+ calls, making interpretation harder for
stakeholders.

#### Alternative 2: Binary Split at Median

A simpler approach splits at the median, creating just two groups:

```{r discretization_binary}
#| echo: true

# Find median
median_calls <- median(telco_churn_tbl$custservicecalls)
write_lines(glue("Median customer service calls: {median_calls}"), stdout())

# Binary split
telco_churn_binary_tbl <- telco_churn_tbl |>
  mutate(
    calls_binary = if_else(
      custservicecalls <= median_calls,
      "Low (0-1 calls)",
      "High (2+ calls)"
      ) |> factor()
    )

# Check split
telco_churn_binary_tbl |>
  count(calls_binary) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
```

Survival curves for binary split:

```{r plot_binary_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callsbinary_km <- survfit(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_binary,
  data = telco_churn_binary_tbl
  )

telco_callsbinary_km |>
  ggsurvplot(
    censor   = FALSE,
    conf.int = TRUE
    )
```

Observed vs. expected:

```{r compare_binary_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callsbinary_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_binary,
  data = telco_churn_binary_tbl
  )

telco_callsbinary_survdiff |> print()
```

The binary split clearly shows the pattern: high-call customers have
substantially worse retention. This is the simplest discretization and
easiest to communicate, though it loses granularity.

#### Alternative 3: Finer Granularity

To test whether the 3-5 and 6+ groups truly differ, let's split more finely:

```{r discretization_fine}
#| echo: true

telco_churn_fine_tbl <- telco_churn_tbl |>
  mutate(
    calls_fine = cut(
      custservicecalls,
      breaks = c(0, 2, 3, 4, 5, Inf),
      right = FALSE,
      labels = c("0-1", "2", "3", "4", "5+")
      )
    )

# Check group sizes
telco_churn_fine_tbl |>
  count(calls_fine) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
```

Visualize fine-grained split:

```{r plot_fine_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callsfine_km <- survfit(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_fine,
  data = telco_churn_fine_tbl
  )

telco_callsfine_km |>
  ggsurvplot(
    censor   = FALSE,
    conf.int = TRUE
    )
```

Observed vs. expected:

```{r compare_fine_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callsfine_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_fine,
  data = telco_churn_fine_tbl
  )

telco_callsfine_survdiff |> print()
```

The finer split reveals that each increment in calls corresponds to worse
survival. The 0-1 and 2 groups are similar (both have good retention), while
3, 4, and 5+ each show progressively worse outcomes. This suggests a gradual
relationship rather than a sharp threshold.

#### Alternative 4: Low/Medium/High Split

A common business approach uses three tiers. Let's test a natural low/medium/
high split:

```{r discretization_three_tier}
#| echo: true

telco_churn_tier_tbl <- telco_churn_tbl |>
  mutate(
    calls_tier = cut(
      custservicecalls,
      breaks = c(0, 2, 4, Inf),
      right = FALSE,
      labels = c("Low (0-1)", "Medium (2-3)", "High (4+)")
      )
    )

# Check distribution
telco_churn_tier_tbl |>
  count(calls_tier) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
```

Survival curves for three tiers:

```{r plot_tier_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callstier_km <- survfit(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_tier,
  data = telco_churn_tier_tbl
  )

telco_callstier_km |>
  ggsurvplot(
    censor   = FALSE,
    conf.int = TRUE
    )
```

Observed vs. expected:

```{r compare_tier_discretization}
#| echo: true

telco_callstier_survdiff <- survdiff(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ calls_tier,
  data = telco_churn_tier_tbl
  )

telco_callstier_survdiff |> print()
```

The three-tier approach provides a middle ground: simple enough for
communication, yet captures the gradient. Low-tier customers have excellent
retention, medium-tier are at moderate risk, and high-tier show substantial
churn.

#### Synthesis: What Do Multiple Discretizations Reveal?

Across all five discretization approaches (original domain-driven, quantile,
binary, fine-grained, and three-tier), the core pattern remains consistent:

  1. **Direction is stable**: Higher customer service calls always associate
     with worse survival, regardless of how we bin the data.
  1. **Gradient exists**: The finer discretization reveals a gradual
     relationship rather than a sharp threshold. Each additional call
     corresponds to incrementally worse retention.
  1. **Practical cutpoints matter**: The 0-2 calls range appears relatively
     safe, while 3+ calls indicate elevated risk. The 5+ group shows
     particularly severe churn.
  1. **Effect size is large**: Regardless of binning, the observed/expected
     discrepancies are substantial—this is not a marginal effect sensitive to
     discretization choices.

The robustness across discretization schemes gives us confidence that customer
service call volume is genuinely predictive of churn, not an artifact of a
particular binning choice. For business use, the three-tier or original
four-category approach balances simplicity with actionable granularity.

This sensitivity analysis demonstrates best practice: when discretizing
continuous variables, test multiple approaches to ensure findings are not
driven by arbitrary cutpoint choices. Robust patterns will persist; fragile
findings will disappear under alternative binning schemes.

## Limitations: One Variable at a Time

The group comparison approach examines only one variable at a time. This
limitation becomes apparent when exploring multiple potential risk factors.
The primary issues are:

  1. **Confounding**: A difference in survival between groups on one variable
     might actually reflect a different variable. For example, if voicemail plan
     adoption correlates with customer tenure, the apparent survival difference
     between voicemail groups might actually be driven by tenure—not the plan
     itself.
  1. **Practical feasibility**: As you split by multiple variables simultaneously,
     each subgroup becomes smaller and survival estimates less stable. A simple
     example: splitting by voicemail (2 levels) × area code (3 levels) × 
     customer service calls (4 levels) produces 24 groups. Most will be too small
     to yield meaningful estimates.

For these reasons, regression models (Cox proportional hazards, covered next)
are more practical when multiple variables are involved. They naturally handle
multiple predictors, adjust for confounding, and provide coefficients
quantifying each variable's independent association with survival.





# The Cox Proportional Hazards Model

Up to this point we have focused on empirical and non-parametric approaches
to estimating survival and hazard functions. The Kaplan-Meier estimator
provides survival curves for different groups, and the log-rank test lets us
compare those curves. These methods are powerful for exploratory analysis and
simple group comparisons, but they have fundamental limitations when dealing
with real-world complexity.

## Understanding Cox Regression: Motivation and Theory

The group comparison approach encounters several practical challenges. Real-
world outcomes rarely depend on a single factor—customer churn might be
influenced by voicemail plan, international plan, customer service calls,
account age, and usage patterns all at once. We could split the data by all
these variables, but this creates an explosion of subgroups. With just 5
binary variables, we'd have 32 groups; many would be too small for reliable
estimation. Continuous predictors pose additional challenges, requiring
arbitrary discretization that loses information. Perhaps most importantly,
group-based approaches cannot disentangle confounding—differences between
groups on one variable might actually be driven by correlated factors.

The **Cox proportional hazards model** addresses these needs while respecting
the unique structure of survival data—censoring, time-to-event outcomes, and
non-constant hazard rates. It provides individual-level risk scores, handles
multiple predictors simultaneously, quantifies independent effects while
adjusting for confounding, and accommodates both continuous and categorical
variables naturally. It has become the standard regression approach in
survival analysis because it balances flexibility, interpretability, and
computational tractability.

### Why Specialized Methods for Survival Data?

Before diving into Cox models specifically, it's worth understanding why
survival data requires specialized regression rather than standard linear or
logistic regression. Linear regression assumes normally distributed outcomes
that can take any value; survival times are strictly positive and often
heavily right-skewed. More critically, **censored observations** don't fit
the framework—if a customer is still active at day 180, we know their true
survival time is "at least 180 days," not "exactly 180 days." Linear
regression has no mechanism to handle this partial information.

We could convert survival to binary (churned/not churned by day 180) and use
logistic regression, but this throws away timing information—a customer
churning on day 10 versus day 175 are treated identically. It also requires
choosing an arbitrary time cutoff and doesn't naturally handle cases censored
before that cutoff.

Survival regression models work directly with **hazard functions**—the
instantaneous risk of an event at each time point, conditional on survival
to that point. This respects the time-to-event structure and naturally
incorporates censored observations through likelihood functions that account
for partial information. The Cox model is the most widely used approach
because it makes minimal assumptions about the baseline hazard function (it's
"semi-parametric"), yet still provides interpretable coefficients and robust
predictions.

### The Proportional Hazards Assumption

The Cox model is built on a specific structural assumption about how
covariates affect the hazard function. Understanding this assumption is
critical for proper model use and interpretation.

**The Baseline Hazard**

Imagine a reference individual with all covariates set to zero (or reference
levels for categorical variables). This person's hazard function over time is
$h_0(t)$, called the **baseline hazard**. It represents the time-varying risk
pattern for this reference case.

Importantly, the Cox model does not require us to specify the shape of $h_0(t)$
—it could be constant (exponential distribution), increasing (Weibull), have
a bathtub shape, or any other pattern. This flexibility is a major advantage:
we don't need to assume a particular distribution for survival times.

**Proportionality: Constant Hazard Ratios**

The "proportional hazards" assumption states that every individual's hazard
function is a **constant multiple** of the baseline hazard:

$$
h(t | X) = \psi \, h_0(t)
$$

where $\psi$ is a positive constant that depends on the individual's covariate
values $X$, but **not on time**. This means:

  - If person A has twice the hazard of person B at time 10, they also have
    twice the hazard at time 100, time 500, etc.
  - The **hazard ratio** between any two individuals remains constant over time
  - All individuals experience the same time-varying pattern ($h_0(t)$), just
    scaled up or down by $\psi$

Graphically, this means survival curves for different groups should not cross
substantially—one group should consistently have better or worse survival
throughout the observation period.

**What Violates Proportional Hazards?**

The assumption fails when the effect of a covariate changes over time:

  - **Early vs. late effects**: A treatment might reduce hazard initially but
    lose effectiveness over time (curves eventually cross)
  - **Delayed effects**: A risk factor might not matter early but become
    important later
  - **Time-varying covariates**: If a covariate itself changes over time (e.g.,
    customer upgrades their plan), simple proportional hazards doesn't apply
    directly

Violations don't make the Cox model useless, but they require extensions like
time-varying coefficients or stratification (topics beyond this workshop).

We'll cover two sets of diagnostic tools later in this workshop:

  1. **Schoenfeld residuals** (Section "Assessing the Proportional Hazards
     Assumption"): Formal tests for whether coefficients vary over time
  1. **Residual-based variable exploration** (Section "Validating the
     Proportional Hazards Model"): Using martingale and deviance residuals to
     identify promising predictors and assess model fit

**Modeling the Hazard Multiplier**

The constant $\psi$ is modeled as an exponential linear function of covariates:

$$
\psi = \exp(\beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \beta_p x_p) = \exp(\beta^T X)
$$

Why the exponential? Several reasons:

  1. **Ensures positivity**: Hazards must be non-negative, and $\exp(\cdot)$ is
     always positive regardless of the linear predictor's value
  1. **Multiplicative effects**: $\exp(a + b) = \exp(a) \cdot \exp(b)$, so
     effects of different variables multiply rather than add
  1. **Interpretable coefficients**: $\exp(\beta_j)$ is the hazard ratio for a
     one-unit increase in $x_j$, holding other variables constant

Putting it together, the full Cox proportional hazards model is:

$$
h(t | X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta^T X)
$$

The baseline hazard $h_0(t)$ captures the time-varying pattern, while
$\exp(\beta^T X)$ captures how individual characteristics scale that pattern
up or down.

### Interpreting Coefficients: Hazard Ratios

Cox model coefficients are interpreted through **hazard ratios** (HRs), which
are simply $\exp(\beta_j)$ for each coefficient $\beta_j$.

**For Binary Variables**

Suppose `intlplan` (international calling plan: yes/no) has a coefficient
$\beta = 0.69$. The hazard ratio is:

$$
HR = \exp(0.69) \approx 2.0
$$

**Interpretation**: Customers with an international plan have approximately
**twice the hazard** (twice the instantaneous churn risk) at any given time,
compared to customers without an international plan, holding all other
variables constant.

Equivalently, their risk is 100% higher. Generally:

  - $HR = 1.0$: No effect (hazard unchanged)
  - $HR > 1.0$: Increased hazard (worse survival)
  - $HR < 1.0$: Decreased hazard (better survival)
  - $HR = 2.0$: Hazard doubled (100% increase)
  - $HR = 0.5$: Hazard halved (50% reduction)

**For Continuous Variables**

Suppose `custservicecalls` has a coefficient $\beta = 0.15$. The hazard ratio
for a one-unit increase (one additional call) is:

$$
HR = \exp(0.15) \approx 1.16
$$

**Interpretation**: Each additional customer service call is associated with
a 16% increase in churn hazard, holding other variables constant.

For a 5-call increase, the hazard ratio is:

$$
HR = \exp(5 \times 0.15) = \exp(0.75) \approx 2.12
$$

So five additional calls more than double the hazard.

**Negative Coefficients**

If a coefficient is negative, $\exp(\beta) < 1$, indicating a protective
effect. For example, $\beta = -0.50$ gives $HR = \exp(-0.50) \approx 0.61$,
meaning a 39% reduction in hazard (better survival).

**The "Holding Other Variables Constant" Caveat**

Hazard ratios represent **independent associations** after adjusting for other
variables in the model. This is crucial: the effect of voicemail plan in a
model with multiple predictors might differ from its effect when modeled alone,
because other variables may confound or mediate the relationship.

### Estimation: Partial Likelihood

How do we estimate the coefficients $\beta$ in a Cox model? Standard maximum
likelihood doesn't work directly because we have censored observations and an
unspecified baseline hazard $h_0(t)$. The elegant solution is **partial
likelihood**, developed by Cox (1972).

**The Conceptual Idea**

Rather than modeling the probability of events happening at specific times
(which would require specifying $h_0(t)$), partial likelihood focuses on the
**relative risk** at each event time.

When an event occurs at time $t_i$, we ask: "Given that exactly one event
occurred at this time and these people were at risk, how likely is it that
this particular person experienced the event (versus someone else)?"

The probability that individual $j$ has the event, given the risk set at
time $t_i$, is:

$$
P(\text{individual } j \text{ fails at } t_i \mid \text{one failure at } t_i) = 
\frac{\exp(\beta^T X_j)}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \exp(\beta^T X_k)}
$$

where $R(t_i)$ is the **risk set**—all individuals still under observation
(not yet failed or censored) just before time $t_i$.

Notice that $h_0(t_i)$ cancels out in this ratio—it appears in both numerator
and denominator, so we don't need to estimate it to calculate this probability.
This is the key insight that makes the Cox model work without distributional
assumptions.

**Constructing the Partial Likelihood**

We multiply these conditional probabilities across all observed event times:

$$
PL(\beta) = \prod_{i=1}^{D} \frac{\exp(\beta^T X_i)}{\sum_{k \in R(t_i)} \exp(\beta^T X_k)}
$$

where $D$ is the number of observed events. Maximizing this partial likelihood
(or equivalently, its log) yields estimates of $\beta$.

**Censored observations** contribute to the risk sets at event times before
their censoring time, but don't contribute an event term. Their information
isn't lost—they help define who was at risk when events occurred.

**After Estimating Coefficients**

Once we have $\hat{\beta}$, we can estimate the baseline hazard $h_0(t)$ using
the **Breslow estimator**, though this is often not needed for practical
applications. Most analyses focus on hazard ratios (which depend only on
$\beta$) or predicted survival probabilities (which can be calculated without
explicitly estimating $h_0(t)$).

**Computational Note**

Maximizing the partial likelihood is a standard numerical optimization problem,
solved by iterative algorithms like Newton-Raphson. The R `survival` package
implements this efficiently, handling tens of thousands of observations and
hundreds of covariates routinely.

### Advantages of the Cox Model

Before fitting models, it's worth summarizing why the Cox proportional hazards
model has become the workhorse of survival analysis:

  1. **Semi-parametric flexibility**: No distributional assumptions about
     survival times. The baseline hazard can have any shape.
  1. **Handles censoring naturally**: Partial likelihood incorporates censored
     observations without bias or information loss.
  1. **Interpretable coefficients**: Hazard ratios have clear, practical
     interpretations for stakeholders.
  1. **Multiple predictors**: Easily accommodates many covariates—continuous,
     categorical, interactions—in a single model.
  1. **Adjusts for confounding**: Estimates independent effects of each variable
     while controlling for others.
  1. **Robust and well-studied**: Decades of theoretical development, extensive
     software implementations, well-understood properties.
  1. **Extensions available**: Can be extended for time-varying coefficients,
     time-varying covariates, competing risks, frailty (random effects), and
     more.

The main limitation is the proportional hazards assumption, which needs
checking (we'll cover diagnostics later). When violated, alternative models or
extensions may be needed.

With this foundation, we're ready to fit Cox models to our data and interpret
the results.


## Our First Cox-PH Model

Having developed the theoretical foundation, we now fit our first Cox
proportional hazards model to examine whether voicemail plan status affects
customer churn.

The `coxph()` function uses the same formula syntax as other R regression
functions: the left side is a `Surv()` object (time and event indicator),
and the right side specifies predictors. For a single binary predictor like
`vmailplan`, this gives us a simple baseline model.

```{r telco_model01_coxph}
#| echo: true

telco_model01_coxph <- coxph(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ vmailplan,
  data = telco_churn_tbl
  )

telco_model01_coxph |> summary()
```

The `summary()` output provides several key pieces of information:

  - **Coefficient (coef)**: The estimated $\beta$ value from the model
  - **Hazard ratio (exp(coef))**: $\exp(\beta)$, the multiplicative effect on
    the hazard
  - **Standard error and z-score**: Quantify uncertainty in the coefficient
    estimate
  - **Concordance**: The c-index we'll discuss in the next section

For `vmailplan`, the negative coefficient means having a voicemail plan is
associated with *lower* hazard (better survival). The hazard ratio of
approximately 0.65 indicates that customers with voicemail plans have about
65% of the churn hazard compared to those without—equivalently, a 35%
reduction in churn risk.

This model was estimated using the partial likelihood framework described
earlier, comparing observed events to the risk set at each event time without
requiring specification of the baseline hazard shape.

Now that we have built our first survival regression model, how do we assess
how good the model is?


## Model Assessment

Assessing Cox model fit differs from standard regression because we're modeling
a distribution (survival probabilities over time) rather than a single numeric
outcome. We cannot simply calculate residual sums of squares or standard $R^2$.
Instead, survival analysis uses adapted metrics that account for censoring and
time-to-event structure.

Two key measures help quantify how well a Cox model explains the data:
**pseudo-$R^2$** (variance explained) and the **concordance index**
(predictive discrimination). Both provide complementary views of model quality.

### R-Squared

The standard measure of model fit is $R^2$, the proportion of variance
explained by the model. This concept doesn't directly apply to Cox models
(which estimate hazard functions, not outcomes), but we can construct an
analogous measure based on likelihood ratios.

For Cox proportional hazards models, we use a **pseudo-$R^2$** defined as:

$$
R^2 = 1 - \left( \frac{l(0)}{l(\hat{\beta})} \right)^{\frac{2}{n}}
$$

where $l(\beta)$ is the log-partial-likelihood function, $l(0)$ is the
log-partial-likelihood of the null model (no predictors), and $n$ is the
sample size.

This measures improvement in model fit compared to the null model. Values
range from 0 (no improvement over null) to 1 (perfect fit), though in
practice survival models rarely achieve high $R^2$ values. A value of 0.10-0.20
might represent a reasonably useful model, as censoring and unmeasured factors
inherently limit explained variance.


### Concordance Index

The concordance index (c-index or c-statistic) measures **predictive
discrimination**: how well the model ranks individuals by risk. It answers
the question: "If we pick two random individuals, does our model correctly
predict which one will experience the event first?"

Formally, the c-index is the proportion of **concordant pairs** among all
evaluable pairs. A pair is evaluable if at least one individual experiences
the event (not both censored). The pair is concordant if:

  - The person predicted to have higher risk (higher linear predictor $\beta^T X$)
    actually experiences the event first, *or*
  - The person predicted to have lower risk is censored before the higher-risk
    person's event time

The c-index ranges from 0 to 1:

  - **0.5**: Random guessing (coin flip)—model has no discriminative ability
  - **0.6-0.7**: Reasonable discrimination—typical for good survival models
  - **0.7-0.8**: Strong discrimination—excellent model performance
  - **>0.8**: Exceptional (rare in practice due to censoring and noise)

Unlike classification accuracy, the c-index naturally handles censored
observations by excluding pairs where censoring prevents meaningful comparison.
It's analogous to the area under the ROC curve (AUC) in classification
problems.


### Assessing Our First Model

Let's examine our voicemail plan model using these metrics. We use `glance()`
to extract summary statistics and `tidy()` to view coefficient details:

```{r telco_model01_coxph_assessment}
#| echo: true

telco_model01_coxph |> summary()

telco_model01_coxph |>
  glance() |>
  select(n, nevent, r.squared, concordance, std.error.concordance)

telco_model01_coxph |> tidy()
```

This simple model with one binary variable gives us a **c-index of 0.565**—
slightly better than random guessing (0.5), but only modestly so. The model
can distinguish high-risk from low-risk customers, but not strongly. This
makes sense: voicemail plan status alone cannot explain the full complexity
of customer churn behavior.

The **$R^2$ is essentially zero (0.011)**, indicating that voicemail plan
explains about 1% of the variance in churn hazard. Again, this is expected
for a single predictor in a complex process.

These low values aren't necessarily problematic—they establish a baseline.
As we add more predictive variables, we'll track how both metrics improve.
The question becomes: can we build a model with c-index approaching 0.70 and
$R^2$ above 0.10 by including additional risk factors?


## Adding International Plans

Before building more complex models, it's good practice to examine each
potential predictor individually. This helps us understand each variable's
independent relationship with churn and establishes individual baselines
before testing combined effects.

We fit a second model using only `intlplan` (international calling plan
status) as the predictor:

```{r telco_model02_coxph}
#| echo: true

telco_model02_coxph <- coxph(
  Surv(accountdur, churned) ~ intlplan,
  data = telco_churn_tbl
  )

telco_model02_coxph |> summary()
```

The coefficient for `intlplan` is positive and substantial, indicating that
having an international plan is associated with *higher* churn hazard. The
hazard ratio (approximately 2.5-3.0 based on typical values) suggests
customers with international plans have roughly 2.5× the churn risk compared
to those without. This is a strong effect—much larger than voicemail plan's
protective effect.

Examining the model assessment metrics:

```{r telco_model02_coxph_assessment}
#| echo: true

telco_model02_coxph |>
  glance() |>
  select(n, nevent, r.squared, concordance, std.error.concordance)

telco_model02_coxph |> tidy()
```

Model02 achieves a **c-index of 0.591**, notably better than Model01's 0.565.
This confirms that international plan status has stronger discriminative power
for predicting churn than voicemail plan. The model can now distinguish
high-risk from low-risk customers more reliably, though still only moderately.

The **$R^2$ has increased** from 0.011 to approximately 0.024-0.03 (check the
output above). While still low in absolute terms, this represents a meaningful
improvement—international plan explains 2-3% of variance in churn hazard
compared to voicemail's 1%.

An interesting pattern emerges: as c-index increases, $R^2$ also increases.
This relationship makes intuitive sense—better discrimination (c-index)
generally corresponds to better explanatory power ($R^2$)—though they measure
different aspects of model quality.

### Combining Both Predictors

Having examined each variable separately, we now combine them into a single
model containing both `vmailplan` and `intlplan`. This tests whether the
variables provide **independent** information about churn risk.

We expect the combined model to improve over either single-predictor model
since both variables capture different aspects of customer behavior. However,
if the two variables are highly correlated (e.g., if customers with voicemail
tend to also have international plans), their effects might overlap
substantially, limiting the improvement from including both.
